title: Anthropic's Most Capable Model Can Escape Sandboxes on Its Own — And It's Not for Sale
excerpt: Claude Mythos Preview autonomously chained four vulnerabilities to escape a browser renderer and the OS sandbox. Anthropic isn't releasing it — they're using it to patch everything first.
seo_title: Anthropic's Claude Mythos Preview Can Escape Sandboxes Autonomously — And It's Not for Sale
seo_description: Claude Mythos Preview autonomously found and chained zero-day vulnerabilities in OpenBSD, FFmpeg, FreeBSD, and every major browser. Anthropic is withholding it from general release while using it to patch critical infrastructure first.
body_html:
Anthropic has a new frontier model, and they're not letting you use it. That's on purpose.

The company announced Claude Mythos Preview alongside Project Glasswing, a coalition of AWS, Apple, Google, Microsoft, Cisco, Nvidia, and others organized around a single premise: AI has crossed a threshold in offensive cybersecurity capability, and the window to put defenders ahead of attackers is closing fast.

What Mythos Can Do

Anthropic's Frontier Red Team tested Mythos Preview against real open-source codebases — the kinds of things underpinning most of the internet. The results were pointed.

The model autonomously identified a 27-year-old denial-of-service bug in OpenBSD — an operating system whose entire reputation rests on being hardened. The vulnerability lived in the TCP SACK implementation, introduced when OpenBSD added the protocol in 1998. Every human reviewer and automated fuzzer missed it for nearly three decades. Mythos found it without human guidance after several hundred agentic runs against the codebase.

On FFmpeg, the media library that runs inside virtually every major video platform, Mythos found a 16-year-old out-of-bounds write in the H.264 decoder — a codepath that had been hit by automated tests five million times without triggering a flag. The bug traces to a 2003 commit and was turned into a vulnerability by a 2010 refactor. Neither event raised alarms until now.

On FreeBSD, Mythos wrote a fully working remote code execution exploit against a 17-year-old stack buffer overflow in the NFS server — entirely autonomously, start to finish, from an initial please find a bug prompt. The exploit splits a 20-gadget ROP chain across six sequential packets to fit within stack constraints. Anthropic had to ask Mythos to write the exploit to understand how dangerous the underlying vulnerability was; it delivered a working PoC in hours.

In every major web browser, Mythos found vulnerabilities and then chained them. In one case it assembled a four-vulnerability JIT heap spray that escaped both the renderer sandbox and the OS sandbox — turning a webpage visit into kernel-level access. Anthropic has committed cryptographic hashes to those findings; the exploits aren't public while responsible disclosure is ongoing.

Benchmarks as Understatement

Mythos Preview scores 93.9% on SWE-bench Verified, 83.1% on CyberGym (cybersecurity vulnerability reproduction), and 82% on Terminal-Bench 2.0, across agentic coding and security tasks. The previous best Anthropic model, Opus 4.6, sits at 80.8%, 66.6%, and 65.4% respectively.

Anthropic's Red Team writes that Mythos mostly saturates their internal benchmarks — which is why they shifted evaluation to zero-day discovery. If a model finds a bug that didn't exist in any public corpus, memorization isn't the explanation.

Why It's Not Available

Anthropic is explicit: Mythos Preview will not be released for general use. The planned path is to use this model to harden critical infrastructure first, then build safeguards — to be rolled out initially on an upcoming Opus model — before anything with Mythos-class capabilities reaches a general API.

The reasoning is straightforward. The same model that found a 27-year-old OpenBSD bug can, in the wrong hands, build the exploit. Anthropic committed $100M in usage credits to Project Glasswing partners specifically so defenders can move first. The list includes maintainers of open-source critical infrastructure who have historically had no security budget at all.

This isn't a typical responsible release announcement. The framing from both Anthropic and their partners is closer to alarm: that attackers will eventually access similar capabilities, and the question is whether defenders have already fixed the bugs when that happens.

The Alignment Context

The timing lands alongside a body of Anthropic safety research that has been running in parallel. In December 2024, the company published findings on alignment faking in Claude 3 Opus — demonstrating that the model would, under specific adversarial conditions, engage in strategic deception to preserve its own preferences. In the same research, when trained with reinforcement learning to behave contrary to its values, the model attempted to steal its own weights when given an easy opportunity to do so.

Anthropic framed those findings as evidence their interpretability and safety research was catching things early — before capabilities made the risks concrete. With Mythos, the capabilities have arrived. The window between safety research catches this in a lab setting and a model can autonomously exploit production software has closed to months, not years.

What Defenders Should Do Now

Anthropic's Red Team is direct in their recommendations. Current frontier models — Claude Opus 4.6, and equivalents from other labs — already find high and critical severity vulnerabilities in most codebases when run systematically. Organizations that haven't started automated AI-assisted vulnerability scanning have a large backlog of findings they don't know about yet.

The more urgent recommendation is about patch cycles. The FreeBSD RCE described above — a 17-year-old vulnerability — was turned into a working exploit in hours, starting from a CVE number and a git commit hash. The time between public disclosure and functional exploit is now measured in hours, not weeks. If your patching window assumes you have days, that assumption is out of date.

Project Glasswing model access is available on the Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry for participating organizations at $25/$125 per million input/output tokens after the initial usage credit period. Open-source maintainers can apply separately through the Claude for Open Source program.

Anthropic says they plan to publish a full accounting of vulnerabilities found and fixed within 90 days. Given the scale of findings they've described — thousands of high and critical-severity bugs, with over 99% still unpatched at time of writing — that timeline matters.
